questions.question: A customer asks whether you use AI to process their data. You paste their email address and purchase history into ChatGPT to draft a personalised marketing email. Under the Privacy Act 2020, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The Privacy Act doesn't apply because ChatGPT is a third-party tool, not your system
You are responsible for how the customer's personal information is handled, including by AI tools you use
As long as the customer has previously bought from you, you can use their data in any AI tool
The Privacy Act only applies to businesses with more than 20 employees

questions.feedback: The Privacy Act 2020 applies to all businesses that handle personal information, regardless of size. When you use an AI tool to process customer data, you remain responsible under the 13 Information Privacy Principles — including how the tool stores, uses, and discloses that data.

questions.question: Your AI marketing tool generates the claim 'We offer the lowest prices in Canterbury.' What should you do before publishing this?

options.text: Publish it — the AI generated it based on data, so it must be accurate
Add a disclaimer saying 'AI-generated claim, not verified'
Verify the claim with evidence, or change it to something you can substantiate
Remove the word 'lowest' and replace it with 'competitive' — no evidence needed for vague terms

questions.feedback: Under the Fair Trading Act, you must have reasonable grounds for any claims your business makes. 'The AI wrote it' is not a defence. With maximum fines increasing to $5M from 2026, fact-checking AI-generated marketing claims is essential. Even vague claims like 'competitive' could require substantiation if challenged.

questions.question: You're choosing an AI tool for your business. Which of the following are important considerations under IPP 12 (cross-border data transfers)? Select all that apply.

options.text: Where the AI tool processes and stores data
Whether the tool has data protection agreements comparable to NZ standards
The colour scheme and branding of the AI tool
Whether business accounts offer stronger privacy protections than free tiers
Whether NZ-hosted options are available

questions.feedback: IPP 12 requires that personal information sent overseas is protected by comparable privacy standards. Key considerations include where data is processed, what protections are in place, whether business accounts offer stronger terms than free tiers, and whether NZ-hosted alternatives exist (such as the AWS NZ region launched in 2025).

questions.question: Under NZ copyright law, who owns the copyright in content generated by an AI tool?

options.text: The AI tool's developer always owns the copyright
Nobody — AI-generated content cannot be copyrighted
The person who made the arrangements necessary for its creation, subject to the tool's terms of service
Copyright is automatically shared equally between the user and the AI company

questions.feedback: NZ is one of few countries that does protect copyright in computer-generated works. Under the Copyright Act 1994, copyright belongs to the person who made the 'arrangements necessary for the creation of the work.' The more active human involvement (detailed prompts, editing, creative decisions), the stronger the claim. However, the AI tool's terms of service may also affect IP ownership.

questions.question: Which of Te Mana Raraunga's Maori Data Sovereignty principles should a business consider when using AI to process data about Maori communities? Select all that apply.

options.text: Rangatiratanga — Maori have the right to govern how data about them is used
Kaitiakitanga — those holding the data have a responsibility to protect and care for it
Manaakitanga — data use should provide benefit back to Maori communities
None of these — data sovereignty only applies to government agencies

questions.feedback: All three principles apply. Te Mana Raraunga's principles are relevant to any organisation — not just government — that handles data about Maori. Rangatiratanga recognises Maori authority over their data, Kaitiakitanga calls for responsible guardianship, and Manaakitanga requires that data use provides reciprocal benefit. Businesses serving Maori communities should consider all six principles.

questions.question: Your business uses fingerprint scanners for staff time tracking. Under the Biometric Processing Privacy Code 2025, what is your compliance deadline?

options.text: The Code doesn't apply to fingerprint scanners, only facial recognition
3 August 2026
Compliance was required immediately when the Code came into force in November 2025
There is no deadline — the Code is voluntary

questions.feedback: The Biometric Processing Privacy Code 2025 came into force on 3 November 2025, with a grace period for compliance until 3 August 2026. It covers all biometric data, including fingerprints, facial recognition, voiceprints, iris scans, and gait analysis — not just facial recognition.

questions.question: You're creating an AI workplace policy for your small business. Which of the following should be included? Select all that apply.

options.text: A list of approved AI tools
Clear rules about what data can and cannot be entered into AI tools
A requirement that all AI-generated content is reviewed by a human before use
A ban on all AI use to eliminate risk
A review schedule to keep the policy current

questions.feedback: An effective AI policy includes approved tools, data handling rules, quality check requirements, and a regular review cycle. Banning AI entirely is not practical — 52% of NZ businesses already identify shadow AI as a problem, which means staff will use AI regardless. A well-designed policy empowers responsible use rather than driving it underground.

questions.question: A staff member at your business accidentally pastes a client's confidential financial records into a free AI chatbot. Which insurance type would most likely cover the resulting data breach costs?

options.text: General liability insurance
Cyber liability insurance
Contents insurance
Public liability insurance

questions.feedback: Cyber liability insurance is designed to cover data breach costs, including notification, forensic investigation, legal fees, and business interruption. With 43% of business leaders identifying accidental employee data exposure via AI as their biggest cyber risk, cyber liability insurance is increasingly important for businesses using AI tools. Professional indemnity insurance may also apply if the breach involves client professional services.
